query: occupational radiation exposure in nuclear power plants and reproductive outcomes such as conception failure, miscarriage, congenital abnormalities

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Effects on the Fetus -- Issues of Concern -- Potential Effects From Fetal Radiation Exposure
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic mutations Ionizing radiation may increase the frequency of naturally occurring genetic mutations; however, it is hard to detect such small mutations when the spontaneously occurring mutations are already high at approximately 10%. Incidence of radiation-induced mutagenesis has been primarily studied in animal and plant models; however, only limited human data is available apart from follow-up observations of atomic bomb survivors offsprings. In consensus, ionizing radiation-induced mutagenesis has not been demonstrated in any human population at any radiation dose. As for non-ionizing radiation from electromagnetic waves from computers, warming blankets, heating pads, microwave communication systems, microwave ovens, cellular phones, household appliances, power lines, and airport screening devices have minimal reproductive risk. The literature concludes that there is no significant evidence for an association between a woman's exposure to such sources and fetal loss or other adverse reproductive outcomes. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Exposure In Pregnancy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The consequence of radiation exposure in fetuses is mostly based on observations rather than based on scientific research. Ethical issues prohibit researching on the fetus. Therefore, most of the data on the impact of radiation on the fetus derives from observations of patients who suffered Japan’s Hiroshima bombing and the Chernobyl nuclear power plant disaster. [1] [2] Based on the observations made from the victims of the high level of radiation exposure, the consequences of radiation exposure can categorize into four broad groups, including pregnancy loss, malformation, developmental delay or retardation, and carcinogenesis. [1] Pregnancy loss most often happens when radiation exposure happens during early gestation (less than two weeks). [2] Malformations of body parts and developmental delays occur during the organogenesis period (2 weeks to 8 weeks) and are dependent on the radiation dose. [2] Below the threshold level of radiation exposure, there is minimal disruption of organogenesis. Above the threshold, the degree of malformation is related to the dose of the radiation. Lastly, carcinogenesis is considered a stochastic effect. In other words, cancer can develop at any level of radiation exposure. However, the probability of developing cancer increases with the increase in the dose of radiation.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Effects on the Fetus -- Issues of Concern -- Potential Consequences of Fetal Radiation
retrieved_docs.content: Evidence for the harmful effects of radiation on fetal development emerges primarily from animal studies and nuclear accidents, like the Chernobyl disaster and the nuclear bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki. During the first 14 days post-conception, the developing embryo is at the highest risk, just before the implantation stage. A mere dose of 0.1Gy is enough to cause a pre-implantation death in mice in the zygotic stage. This occurs as an "all or none" phenomenon when a direct exposure of as little as 0.15-0.2 Gy at the pre-implantation stage can cause embryonic death. However, should the embryo survives, there is no increase in malformations, given that they are not exposed to more radiation later.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Effects on the Fetus -- Issues of Concern -- Potential Consequences of Fetal Radiation
retrieved_docs.content: The second trimester (16 to 25 weeks) has risks similar to those in the first trimester, such as malformations, growth and mental retardation, cataracts, sterility, and malignancy; however, the risk is much lower. Mental retardation was reportedly as low as 2%, even with a maximum dose of 0.49 Gy. Miller et al. reported microcephaly in 2 out of 30 children exposed to doses lower than 0.1 Gy, and 2 out of 44 children exposed to 0.1- 0.5 Gy, for pregnant mothers who had exposure post-atomic bombings during their second trimester of the period of gestation. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Effects on the Fetus -- Introduction -- Fetal Risks From Ionizing Radiation
retrieved_docs.content: Significant potential harmful effects of ionizing radiation can be summarised into four main categories: Pregnancy loss (miscarriage, stillbirth) Malformation Disturbances of growth or development Mutagenic and carcinogenic effects

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Effects on the Fetus -- Clinical Significance -- Pre-Conceptional Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: No noticeable risks of transmitting radiation-induced abnormalities to offspring from either irradiated parent's gonads before conception have been identified. Studies involving the atomic bomb survivors' children and grandchildren have not described any heritable effects after parental radiation exposure. Recent studies involving childhood cancer survivors treated with radiation therapy have also not demonstrated transmittable genetic effects on their offspring. The offsprings of fathers who receive testicular radiation do not appear to be at increased risk of adverse outcomes. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Effects on the Fetus -- Issues of Concern -- Potential Effects From Fetal Radiation Exposure
retrieved_docs.content: Growth restriction Follow-up data from atomic bomb survivors exhibited a permanent physical growth restriction with increasing radiation dose, significantly above 1 Gy. This was most obvious when the exposure occurred in the first trimester. A 3% to 4% decrease in height at age 18 occurred whenever the cumulative dose was higher than 1 Gy.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Exposure In Pregnancy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Occupational radiation exposure for a pregnant employee should be monitored to make sure the total amount of radiation exposure is under the regulatory limit. According to the National Council of Radiation Protection and Measurement (NCRP), the total dose equivalent to the embryo/fetus should not exceed 500 mRem during the length of the pregnancy. It should not exceed 50mRem in any month during pregnancy. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Effects on the Fetus -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Discussion if the pregnancy should be terminated

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Effects on the Fetus -- Issues of Concern -- Potential Effects From Fetal Radiation Exposure
retrieved_docs.content: Malformations The risk of malformations is higher in early pregnancy during the organogenesis period (2 to 8 weeks). For a fetus under 16 weeks of gestation, the threshold for possible prenatal radiation effects is approximately 0.10 to 0.20 Gy (100 to 200 mGy, 10 to 20 rads). This threshold is much higher after 16 weeks of gestation, at least 0.50 to 0.70 Gy (500 to 700 mGy, 50 to 70 rads). After approximately 20 to 25 weeks of gestation, which is about late in the second trimester, the fetus is relatively resistant to ionizing radiation's teratogenic effects. [22] Brent reported frequent malformations in neonates born to mothers who underwent abdominopelvic irradiation in which the dose exceeded 0.5 Gy. [23] Commonly occurring malformation was microcephaly with significant mental retardation. Since these studies were carried in Japan atomic bomb survivors, the neonatal malformations' curve might differ slightly in those receiving radiation in a standard clinical setting.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Effects on the Fetus -- Introduction -- Fetal Risks From Ionizing Radiation
retrieved_docs.content: Childhood malignancy in the context of prenatal diagnostic and assessment X-ray was first reported by Giles et al. in 1956. [10] Their survey of childhood cancers established that the risk increased linearly with the number of films exposed. The relative risk of developing a childhood cancer-associated was significantly higher if the exposure was during the first trimester, about 2.5 times greater than the third trimester. This study became the working model of various radiation-induced teratogenesis studies. A defining study was by Kato et al . , where they followed up the survivors of the Hiroshima and Nagasaki atomic bombs. [11] It was the most extensive cohort study of intrauterine radiation exposure; interestingly, only 2 cases had childhood cancer before the 14th birthday out of 1630 children exposed without a single case of leukemia. Broadly, radiation effects are expressed as being either deterministic or stochastic.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Effects on the Fetus -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The developing fetus is most sensitive to ionizing radiation harmful effects during the first 14 days post-conception. In this period, either the pregnancy withstands the radiation exposure unharmed or is resorbed, often termed as an "all or none" phenomenon. [13] However, significant consequential damage is seen when exposed during the period of organogenesis (approximately 2 to 8 weeks post-conception or 4 to 10 weeks after the last menstrual period). The embryo may sustain damage due to radiation-induced cell death leading to irregularities in cell migration and proliferation or mitotic delay. Significant sequelae of radiation-induced damage are fetal growth restriction and congenital malformations, particularly of the central nervous system seen as microcephaly and ocular abnormalities, often associated with intellectual disability. Microcephaly is the most frequently seen manifestation of radiation injury in utero.

retrieved_docs.title: Antepartum Care in the Second and Third Trimester -- Other Issues -- Work accommodations
retrieved_docs.content: With more than half of pregnant women working from conception until delivery, employment during the antepartum course is another common area of concern for patients. According to the Family and Medical Leave Act, pregnant employees must be granted at least 12 weeks of unpaid leave from employment for delivery and newborn care. ACOG also states that pregnant women may continue employment until labor begins in the absence of obstetric complications. However, some types of work require accommodations during pregnancy. Especially in jobs with exposures to substances that may result in fetal anomaly, miscarriage, or other adverse effects (eg, lead, mercury, arsenic, pesticides, solvents, ionizing radiation, and specific chemotherapeutic agents), adequate ventilation and protective gear should be ensured. Clinicians should provide appropriate employer letters as needed to obtain work accommodations or maternity leave. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Effects on the Fetus -- Issues of Concern -- Potential Effects From Fetal Radiation Exposure
retrieved_docs.content: Mental retardation As described earlier, studies demonstrated that the risk of mental retardation and microcephaly was most evident when the exposure occurred at 8 to 15 weeks post-conception. The abnormalities were associated with aberrant neuronal development, presumably due to radiation-induced irreversible cell injury, alteration in cellular differentiation, and impaired neuronal migration. No severe intellectual disability cases were seen in newborns of survivors exposed before 8 weeks or after 25 weeks post-conception. The risk emerged as a linear function of dose exposed, with a threshold of 0.12 Gy (120 mGy, 12 rads) at 8 to 15 weeks, and 0.21 Gy (210 mGy, 21 rads) at 16 to 25 weeks. [24] In terms of IQ parameters, the average IQ loss at 8 to 15 weeks is approximately 25 to 31 points per Gy above 0.1 Gy (100 mGy, 10 rads) with a 40% risk of developing severe intellectual disability per Gy above 0.1 Gy. In contrast, the average IQ loss at 16 to 25 weeks was approximately 13 to 21 points per Gy at doses above 0.7 Gy (700 mGy, 70 rads) with a small 9% risk of developing severe intellectual disability per Gy above 0.7 Gy. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Exposure In Pregnancy -- Magnetic Resonance
retrieved_docs.content: MRI uses a magnetic field to generate diagnostic images and does not contribute to ionizing radiation.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Exposure In Pregnancy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The effect of radiation exposure during pregnancy also depends on the gestational age of the fetus. The embryo/fetus is most susceptible to radiation during organogenesis (2 to 7 weeks gestational age) and in the first trimester. The fetus is more resistant to the radiation during the second and third trimester. Dose between 0.05 to 0.5 Gy is generally considered safe for the fetus during the second and third trimester while it is considered potentially harmful during the 1st-trimester fetus. Even though the fetus is more resistant to the radiation during the second and third trimester, a high dose of radiation (greater than 0.5 Gy or 50 rad) may result in adverse effects including miscarriage, growth reduction, IQ reduction, and severe mental retardation. Therefore, clinicians and radiologists should counsel the pregnant patient regardless of the gestational age. [3]

Collectively, the relationships suggest that hormonal prophylaxis, careful assessment of obstetric history, management of maternal asthma, and mitigation of environmental pollutants are important components of comprehensive care aimed at reducing spontaneous abortion and its downstream neonatal complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2527
retrieved_docs.content: The FDA Center for Devices and Radiological Health Terminology includes the medical concepts of إجهاض فائت (missed abortion) and العقم (infertility) within its subset, indicating that these reproductive health conditions are recognized and standardized in the FDA’s terminology framework for devices and radiological health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2759
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is regarded as an allowable therapeutic option in several specific clinical contexts. It is permitted for patients presenting with وضعية المَجيء المقعدي, for individuals who have thyroid disorders, and in situations of تهديد بالإجهاض (threatened abortion). This indicates that radiotherapy is not contraindicated in these particular conditions and may be considered appropriate when clinical judgment deems it necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1719
retrieved_docs.content: Infertility in women of cervical origin is classified as a specific type of cervical disorder (اضطراب في عنق الرحم). This condition is a subset of the broader category of female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث). Female infertility is closely related to endometrial implantation abnormalities (انتباذ بطاني رحمي), indicating that disturbances in the uterine lining can contribute to the inability to conceive. Radiotherapy (radioterapie) is recognized as an approved therapeutic option for cervical disorders and for endometrial implantation abnormalities; in certain clinical scenarios where these pathologies underlie female infertility, radiotherapy may be considered as part of the management strategy. Understanding the hierarchical relationship between cervical‑origin infertility, general female infertility, and endometrial implantation, as well as the role of radiotherapy, helps clinicians tailor comprehensive treatment plans for patients experiencing infertility.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1748
retrieved_docs.content: The knowledge base defines four primary qualifiers—prevention and control, radiotherapy, therapy, and veterinary—each of which can be applied to a wide spectrum of clinical entities. Prevention and control measures are indicated for conditions such as تمزق (tear), اضطراب المريء (esophageal disorder), ناسور مَثانِيٌّ مَهْبِلِيّ (chronic colonic fistula), وضعية المَجيء المقعدي (sitting position), other complications of pregnancy, حَصاة (kidney stone), respiratory failure/insufficiency/arrest in adults, thyroid disorders, انسداد الحالب (ureteral obstruction), الاِختِنَاق (eclampsia), تهديد بالإجهاض (threatened abortion), and نزيف دموي تحت الجافية (subdural hemorrhage). The same set of conditions—except that veterinary considerations are uniquely linked to حَصاة (kidney stone) in human medicine—are also eligible for radiotherapeutic intervention, reflecting the inclusion of radiotherapy as a treatment option for kidney stones, congenital nervous system anomalies, respiratory failure,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Infertility in women of cervical origin is classified as a disorder of the cervix and is recognized as a specific subtype of female infertility. Radiotherapy is an allowed therapeutic qualifier for disorders of the cervix, female infertility, endometrial implantation, and mitral valve stenosis, and it can be qualified by a surgeon (قساح). Moreover, female infertility is related to endometrial implantation, indicating a pathophysiological connection between these reproductive conditions. This integrated view underscores how cervical‑origin infertility fits within broader gynecologic disorders and how radiotherapy, potentially overseen by a surgeon, may be applied across both gynecologic and cardiac conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2692
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is recognized as an allowable therapeutic option for both female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) and uterine endometriosis (انتباذ بطاني رحمي). These two gynecologic conditions are interrelated, as uterine endometriosis is a known contributor to infertility in women. Consequently, radiotherapy may be considered within the broader management strategies for these linked disorders, reflecting their clinical connection and the potential role of radiotherapeutic interventions in addressing both conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1532
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are uterine conditions that arise within the uterus and are clinically associated with optional termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري). This relationship underscores that these complications are localized to the uterine environment and may influence or be influenced by decisions regarding elective pregnancy termination, highlighting the importance of uterine health in the management of pregnancy‑related problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1497
retrieved_docs.content: The FDA Center for Devices and Radiological Health Terminology includes the reproductive health concepts إجهاض فائت (missed abortion) and العقم (infertility) within its defined subset of terms. In this framework, إجهاض فائت is linked to ولادة جنين ميت (birth of a dead fetus), reflecting the clinical relationship between a fetal death that remains in utero and the eventual delivery of a non‑viable infant. Recognizing both إجهاض فائت and العقم in the FDA terminology underscores their importance for device regulation, diagnostic classification, and the management of patients experiencing these reproductive disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are clinically associated with إنهاء الحمل – اختياري (optional termination of pregnancy) and also have a relationship with اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder). This indicates that uterine disorders are considered among the other complications of pregnancy and that optional termination of pregnancy can be clinically linked to these pregnancy‑related complications.

Therapeutic interventions (the “therapy” qualifier) are applicable to حَصاة (kidney stone), congenital nervous system anomalies, respiratory failure/insufficiency/arrest in adults, thyroid disorders, ureteral obstruction, eclampsia, threatened abortion, and subdural hemorrhage, and the “allowed qualifier” is used to denote these therapy‑eligible diagnoses in both human and veterinary contexts. In veterinary medicine, the veterinary qualifier extends to the same list of conditions—including nervous system congenital anomalies, adult respiratory failure, thyroid disorders, ureteral obstruction, eclampsia, threatened abortion, and subdural hemorrhage—underscoring its role in cross‑species disease

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2166
retrieved_docs.content: Intrauterine pregnancy is clinically linked to several obstetric interventions and outcomes. It often leads to the use of labor induction (تحفيز المخاض) to initiate delivery when medically indicated, and in some cases delivery may be performed with forceps (الولادة بالملقط) to assist the birth process. Although intrauterine pregnancy itself signifies successful conception, certain underlying conditions or complications associated with the pregnancy can also be related to subsequent infertility (العقم), highlighting the need for careful evaluation of reproductive health before and after pregnancy. Understanding these interconnections helps clinicians anticipate appropriate labor management strategies and address potential fertility concerns in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1748
retrieved_docs.content: system congenital anomalies, adult respiratory failure, thyroid disorders, ureteral obstruction, eclampsia, threatened abortion, and subdural hemorrhage—underscoring its role in cross‑species disease classification. Overall, the framework integrates preventive, radiotherapeutic, therapeutic, and veterinary perspectives to standardize disease description and guide management across diverse human and animal health conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2316
retrieved_docs.content: A poor reproductive history, reflecting previous obstetric complications or adverse outcomes, is clinically associated with an increased risk of preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). This connection highlights the importance of detailed reproductive assessments in prenatal care to identify women who may be more likely to experience early delivery and to apply targeted monitoring and preventive strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2007
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy observable is employed as an interpretive tool for إجهاض معتاد, indicating that observable signs and findings during pregnancy can be used to recognize or assess the condition of recurrent miscarriage. This relationship underscores the clinical importance of monitoring pregnancy‑related observations to identify and manage habitual abortion effectively.
